meta.courseTitle: Applied Nursing Practice — Skills, Pharmacology, Mental Health

tasks.chapterTitle: Chapter 1: Overview of Management and Professional Issues
Chapter 7: Collaboration Within the Interprofessional Team

messages.content:
This summary covers the essential professional and management concepts outlined in the "Nursing Management and Professional Concepts 2e" textbook for prelicensure students.

### **Core Overview of the RN Role**
The Registered Nurse (RN) role is governed by two primary American Nurses Association (ANA) documents:
*   **Code of Ethics for Nurses:** Sets the ethical framework for all nursing practice [S.29].
*   **Nursing: Scope and Standards of Practice:** Defines the who, what, where, when, why, and how of the profession [S.29].

**Key Definition:** According to the ANA, nursing is the integration of the art and science of caring. It involves the diagnosis and treatment of human responses to health issues and the active advocacy for individuals, families, and communities [S.29].

---

### **Key Professional Competencies**
To prepare students for professional practice and the NCLEX-RN, the curriculum focuses on several critical areas:

*   **Leadership and Management:** Developing skills in delegation, supervision, and acting as an interprofessional team leader [S.6, S.11].
*   **Clinical Judgment and Prioritization:** Utilizing tools for decision-making and efficient time management to ensure safety and quality care [S.5].
*   **Legal & Ethical Practice:** Understanding the nurse's legal liability, the State Board of Nursing (SBON) regulations, and navigating complex ethical dilemmas via ethics committees [S.6, S.7].
*   **Collaboration:** Adopting interprofessional core competencies, engaging in effective team communication, and utilizing conflict resolution strategies [S.7].
*   **Health Care Economics:** Analyzing reimbursement models, navigating budgets/staffing, and practicing resource stewardship [S.7, S.8].
*   **Quality and Advocacy:** Applying evidence-based practice (EBP) to improve patient outcomes and advocating for policy and patient safety (utilizing QSEN competencies) [S.8, S.9].

---

### **Preparation and Sustainability**
*   **Transition to Practice:** The text emphasizes preparing for licensure, applying for positions, and embracing the role of a "lifelong learner" [S.9].
*   **Burnout Prevention:** A dedicated focus is placed on identifying and mitigating professional stress in oneself and others through self-care models to support retention within the health care system [S.9].

### **Educational Approach**
This resource uses:
*   **Clinical Judgment:** Uses "unfolding case studies" reflective of the NCSBN Clinical Judgment Measurement Model [S.11, S.23].
*   **Evidence-Based Practice:** Content is grounded in scholarly research and is regularly updated to reflect new guidelines and NCLEX-RN test plans [S.11, S.23].
*   **Holism:** Emphasizes addressing social determinants of health (SDOH) to provide culturally responsive, patient-centered care [S.23].

Lesson 5 — Nursing Management - Presentation4 slides
Nursing Management & Professional Concepts
  • Comprehensive guide for prelicensure nursing students.
  • Aligned with 2023 NCLEX-RN and state practice standards.

### Summary: Nursing Management & Professional Concepts (2nd Edition)

The provided text serves as an introduction to the second edition of the *Nursing Management & Professional Concepts* Open Educational Resource (OER) textbook, designed for prelicensure nursing students preparing for the NCLEX-RN.

**Core Purpose and Standards**
The text is aligned with the Wisconsin Technical College System (WTCS) curriculum, the 2023 NCLEX-RN Test Plan, and the Wisconsin Nurse Practice Act. It provides a comprehensive foundation for students transitioning into the professional registered nurse (RN) role.

**Key Subject Areas**
The curriculum addresses essential professional nursing competencies, including:
*   Leadership, management, and delegation strategies.
*   Legal and ethical frameworks for practice.
*   Interprofessional collaboration and advocacy.
*   Healthcare economics, quality improvement, and evidence-based practice.
*   Strategies for professional longevity, specifically identifying and preventing burnout through self-care.

**Educational Features**
To facilitate active learning and clinical judgment, the book includes:
*   Interactive, online learning activities.
*   NCLEX Next Generation-style case studies.
*   A "suite of patients" in the Appendix to support classroom discussion and real-world application.

**Development and Accessibility**
This resource is part of the Open RN series, funded by the U.S. Department of Education and WisTech Open. It is peer-reviewed, evidence-based, and licensed under CC BY 4.0, allowing for free online access or downloadable formats. Notably, the content was developed through expert analysis rather than artificial intelligence to ensure scholarly integrity.

### Summary: Preface and Front Matter

This preface introduces the second edition of the textbook *Nursing Management and Professional Concepts*, an open educational resource (OER) developed by the Open RN Project.

**Key Contributors**
* Editors: Kimberly Ernstmeyer and Dr. Elizabeth Christman led the development team. 
* Authors and Contributors: The content was created through a collaborative effort involving nursing faculty, industry professionals, and librarians across various technical colleges and healthcare systems.
* Advisory and Review: An extensive advisory committee and a large group of peer reviewers ensured the content aligns with current industry practices, evidence-based guidelines, and the 2023 NCLEX nursing test plans.

**Developmental Basis**
The second edition incorporates feedback from Wisconsin Technical College System (WTCS) faculty and reflects updates to the statewide nursing curriculum. It is designed to be a living resource that evolves based on user feedback.

**Usage and Licensing**
* License: The textbook is available under a Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International (CC-BY) license. This allows users to share and adapt the material freely, provided they give appropriate attribution to the original authors and the WisTech Open project.
* Feedback: The editors explicitly request that users report course adoptions, submit feedback, or request corrections through the provided online forms at wistechopen.org.

**Attribution and References**
The text integrates content from various open educational resources, including existing nursing texts and medical literature. All non-original content is cited in footnotes, and a standard attribution statement is provided for all other original content developed for this volume.

### Summary of Standards and Conceptual Approach in Open RN

The *Nursing Management and Professional Concepts* textbook is structured around professional nursing standards and a core set of educational concepts to ensure competency, safety, and equity in healthcare.

**1. External Standards and Frameworks**
The curriculum is aligned with several influential organizations to ensure that graduates meet national and professional expectations:
* **Professional Practice:** Adheres to the American Nurses Association (ANA) Standards of Practice and Code of Ethics.
* **Licensure Alignment:** Content is mapped to the National Council Licensure Examination (NCLEX-RN) Test Plans to ensure entry-level competency.
* **Educational Competencies:** Incorporates guidelines from the National League for Nursing (NLN) and the American Association of Colleges of Nursing (AACN).
* **Quality and Safety:** Utilizes Quality and Safety Education for Nurses (QSEN) competencies, including patient-centered care, teamwork, and informatics.
* **Regulatory and Public Health Goals:** Reflects the Wisconsin Administrative Code (Chapter N6) and supports the objectives of Healthy People 2030 regarding population health and social determinants.

**2. Conceptual Approach**
The textbook employs pedagogical strategies designed to bridge the gap between theory and clinical practice:
* **Holism:** Emphasizes wellness and the environmental factors affecting health, including the social determinants of health (SDOH).
* **Clinical Judgment:** Uses case studies modeled after the NCSBN Clinical Judgment Measurement Model to guide students through cognitive processes like analyzing cues and generating solutions.
* **Evidence-Based Practice (EBP):** Integrates current research via hyperlinks and footnotes to promote digital literacy and ensure students remain current with updated NCLEX standards.
* **Inclusivity and Accessibility:** Features clear, inclusive language (including the singular "they"), open-source imagery under Creative Commons, and open pedagogy that invites student contributions.
* **Cultural Humility:** Encourages a practice framework rooted in providing culturally responsive care to diverse populations.

**3. Supplementary Resources**
To support diverse learning needs, the textbook provides:
* Interactive learning activities and formative feedback tools.
* Next Generation NCLEX-style case studies.
* Supplementary educational videos and offline-accessible versions of the text.

### Summary: Management and Professional Issues in Nursing

This section outlines the regulatory and professional framework governing registered nurses (RNs) in the United States, primarily guided by the American Nurses Association (ANA).

**Professional Frameworks**
The ANA provides two foundational resources:
*   **The Code of Ethics for Nurses with Interpretive Statements:** Establishes the ethical framework for all nursing roles and settings.
*   **Nursing: Scope and Standards of Practice:** Defines the professional "who, what, where, when, why, and how" of nursing, ensuring competent care through the nursing process and professional behaviors.

**Legal and Regulatory Governance**
Nurses must operate within several layers of authority:
*   **Nurse Practice Acts (NPA):** State-specific laws that define the legal scope of practice. State Boards of Nursing enforce these acts; failure to comply can result in disciplinary action, including license suspension or revocation.
*   **Agency Policies, Procedures, and Protocols:** Internal institutional documents that dictate specific workflows. These supersede nursing school instruction; failing to adhere to these can lead to legal liability and jeopardize professional licenses.
*   **Federal Regulations:** Organizations like The Joint Commission and the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) set national standards for safety, quality, and reimbursement. CMS regulations, for example, enforce clinical safety measures such as mandatory medication administration checks.

**Standards of Professional Practice**
The ANA categorizes professional expectations into two distinct types:
1.  **Standards of Practice:** Focus on clinical competence utilizing the nursing process: assessment, diagnosis, outcomes identification, planning, implementation, and evaluation.
2.  **Standards of Professional Performance:** Focus on professional conduct, including ethics, advocacy, collaboration, leadership, education, and resource stewardship.

**Core Obligation**
Nurses hold a social contract to protect, promote, and optimize health. It is the individual responsibility of each RN to stay informed of their specific state regulations and organizational policies to protect their practice and ensure patient safety.

### Summary of Prioritization and Professional Nursing Practice

The provided text outlines the core standards for registered nurses and introduces foundational concepts for nursing prioritization in complex, high-demand healthcare environments.

#### Professional Practice Standards
Nursing practice is grounded in professional standards that ensure quality and ethics. Key areas include:
*   **Ethics and Advocacy:** Integrating ethical decision-making into all practice and demonstrating advocacy for clients across all settings.
*   **Communication and Collaboration:** Practicing effective communication and interprofessional collaboration to ensure client safety and resolve conflicts.
*   **Leadership and Education:** Providing leadership within the profession, engaging in continuous professional development, and pursuing specialty certification.
*   **Scholarly Inquiry and Quality:** Utilizing evidence-based practice and research to contribute to quality improvement initiatives.
*   **Evaluation and Stewardship:** Evaluating nursing practice (including legal and ethical implications) and managing resources (financial and environmental) efficiently and responsibly.

#### Prioritization and Time Management
In contemporary healthcare, nurses frequently face "time scarcity"—a state where the demand for care exceeds available resources, leading to potential stress, burnout, and reduced patient safety. To manage these pressures, nurses must utilize specific organizational tools:

*   **Acuity vs. Intensity:** 
    *   **Acuity** refers to the medical severity of a client's condition (e.g., hemodynamic instability, oxygen requirements, or high-risk medications), necessitating more frequent clinical assessment.
    *   **Intensity** refers to the time and labor required for direct care (e.g., ADLs, wound management, education, or psychosocial support).
*   **Staffing Equity:** Organizations use classification systems (often with ratings from 1 to 4) to quantify acuity and intensity, ensuring that nursing assignments are balanced to maintain safe, equitable care.
*   **Clinical Judgment:** Nurses must combine situational awareness and formal frameworks to distinguish between urgent crises (e.g., patient falls or critical laboratory values) and routine care needs (e.g., planned teaching).

Effective prioritization is a cultivated skill that bridges the transition from novice to competent practitioner, essential for mitigating professional exhaustion and optimizing clinical outcomes.

### Summary of Part II and Part III (Prioritization, Delegation, and Supervision)

This section provides critical frameworks for managing nursing responsibilities, clinical decision-making, and professional teamwork.

#### Chapter 2: Prioritization
Effective prioritization is essential for safe patient care and time management. Key concepts include:

*   **Tenets of Prioritization:** Nurses must distinguish between urgent and non-urgent tasks, often using frameworks like the ABCs (Airway, Breathing, Circulation) and Maslow’s Hierarchy of Needs to determine the order of interventions.
*   **Critical Thinking and Clinical Reasoning:** These processes allow nurses to synthesize data and anticipate patient needs. Clinical reasoning is the application of knowledge to specific patient outcomes, while critical thinking involves evaluating evidence to make sound decisions.
*   **Time Management:** Organizing tasks, setting goals, and eliminating distractions allow for the completion of necessary care without compromising patient safety.

#### Chapter 3: Delegation and Supervision
The ability to appropriately assign and delegate tasks is a core leadership function for the Registered Nurse (RN).

*   **Communication:** Clear, concise communication, often utilizing tools like SBAR (Situation, Background, Assessment, Recommendation), is the foundation of successful interprofessional collaboration and delegation.
*   **Assignment vs. Delegation:** An assignment involves the distribution of work that each staff member is already qualified and licensed to perform. Delegation involves transferring the authority to perform a specific nursing task to a competent individual while the delegating nurse retains accountability for the outcome.
*   **The Five Rights of Delegation:** To ensure safe practice, nurses must follow these established rights:
    1. Right Task
    2. Right Circumstance
    3. Right Person
    4. Right Direction/Communication
    5. Right Supervision/Evaluation
*   **Supervision:** After delegating, the RN is responsible for monitoring the task’s progress, providing feedback, and evaluating the patient's response. The RN cannot delegate tasks that require complex clinical judgment, specialized assessment, or nursing diagnosis.

This section, titled "Leadership and Management" (Part IV, Chapter 4), provides a foundational overview of the roles and responsibilities required of professional nurses in healthcare settings. It focuses on the distinction between leadership and management, as well as the strategies necessary to lead effectively within an evolving healthcare system.

Key Takeaways:

Leadership vs. Management
Leadership represents the ability to influence, motivate, and enable others to contribute toward the effectiveness and success of the organizations of which they are members. It is fundamentally about people and relationships. In contrast, management focuses on the coordination of resources, planning, organizing, and controlling systems to achieve organizational goals. Effective nursing practice requires a synergy of both skill sets.

Basic Concepts
The chapter outlines various leadership theories and styles, such as servant leadership, transformational leadership, and transactional leadership. It emphasizes that professional nursing requires constant development of soft skills, such as emotional intelligence, communication, and conflict resolution, to foster a positive professional environment.

Implementing Change
A significant portion of the text is dedicated to change management. Because healthcare is dynamic, nurses must be prepared to participate in and lead change initiatives. The text introduces common models for implementing evidence-based change, highlighting the need for stakeholder buy-in, clear communication, and the monitoring of outcomes to ensure that changes lead to sustainable quality improvement.

Professional Application
The goal of this chapter is to prepare nurses to transition from the student role to the professional Registered Nurse (RN) role. By mastering these concepts, graduates can better navigate complex clinical environments, advocate for safer patient care, and take on formal or informal leadership roles within an interprofessional team.

The chapter concludes with spotlight applications and learning activities designed to reinforce these concepts through practical, case-based scenarios.

### Summary of Part V: Legal Implications (Chapters 5.1–5.8)

This section examines the complex legal environment in which nurses practice, focusing on regulatory compliance, professional liability, and the protection of licensure. 

**Key Concepts and Legal Frameworks**
The legal system for nursing is structured through state-specific statutes, such as the Nurse Practice Act. These laws define the scope of practice, set standards for professional conduct, and establish the requirements for maintaining a nursing license.

**Professional Liability and Accountability**
Nurses are accountable for their actions under civil and criminal law. Liability often arises from a failure to meet the standard of care. Key legal concerns include:
* Professional Liability: Understanding that nurses are personally responsible for their actions and the outcomes of their nursing decisions.
* Frequent Allegations: Common legal issues include medication errors, documentation failures, and breaches of patient confidentiality.
* SBON Investigations: The State Board of Nursing (SBON) holds the authority to investigate allegations. A nurse's failure to adhere to the standard of care can lead to disciplinary actions, ranging from reprimands to the permanent revocation of a nursing license.

**Risk Mitigation and License Protection**
Protecting a license requires proactive adherence to professional standards and institutional policies. Important protective practices include:
* Thorough, accurate, and timely documentation of all nursing interventions and patient assessments.
* Maintaining clinical competence through continuous education.
* Understanding institutional protocols and chain-of-command reporting to ensure patient safety and legal compliance.
* Recognizing the legal boundaries between different levels of nursing practice (e.g., RN vs. LPN roles).

**Overall Governance**
The primary objective of these legal structures is the protection of the public. By adhering to the legal standards of practice and maintaining ethical responsibility, nurses minimize their risk of legal action and ensure the delivery of safe, high-quality care.

### Summary of Chapter 6: Ethical Practice (Pages 259–316)

This chapter addresses the foundational role of ethics in professional nursing, focusing on how nurses identify, evaluate, and navigate moral challenges in clinical settings.

**Basic Ethical Concepts**
Nursing practice is grounded in professional ethical standards, such as those outlined by the American Nurses Association (ANA) Code of Ethics. Core principles include:
* Autonomy: Respecting the patient's right to self-determination.
* Beneficence: Acting in the best interest of the patient.
* Nonmaleficence: The duty to "do no harm."
* Justice: Ensuring fairness in the distribution of resources and care.
* Fidelity, Veracity, and Confidentiality: Maintaining trust, honesty, and privacy in the nurse-patient relationship.

**Navigating Ethical Dilemmas**
An ethical dilemma occurs when two or more ethical principles conflict, with no single clear solution. The process for resolution involves:
1. Identifying the conflicting values or principles.
2. Gathering relevant facts and clinical data.
3. Exploring all possible approaches and their consequences.
4. Implementing the most ethically sound decision while providing support to all involved parties.

**Ethics Committees and Student Considerations**
Hospitals utilize interprofessional ethics committees to provide consultation on complex cases where moral consensus is difficult to reach. These committees help facilitate communication between the healthcare team, the patient, and the family. Furthermore, nursing students must recognize that they are bound by the same ethical codes as licensed professionals. Even during clinical rotations, students are expected to engage in ethical decision-making, maintain patient confidentiality, and advocate for safe, quality care.

**Application**
The chapter emphasizes that ethical practice is not merely theoretical but a daily requirement for clinical judgment. By utilizing the provided spotlight applications and learning activities, students practice applying these moral frameworks to realistic case scenarios to prepare for the complexities of the registered nurse role.

### Summary: Chapter 7 - Collaboration Within the Interprofessional Team (Pages 317–430)

This chapter focuses on the necessity of effective collaboration among diverse healthcare professionals to improve patient outcomes and ensure safety. 

**Key Concepts:**

**Interprofessional Education Collaborative (IPEC) Core Competencies**
The framework for effective teamwork relies on four core competencies: values and ethics, roles and responsibilities, interprofessional communication, and teams and teamwork. These competencies ensure that practitioners from different disciplines can work together while respecting each other's unique contributions.

**Roles and Responsibilities**
Clear identification of each team member's scope of practice, expertise, and responsibilities is essential. Understanding these roles minimizes duplication of effort, prevents gaps in care, and allows for the optimization of the patient's care plan.

**Communication Strategies**
Effective interprofessional communication is the cornerstone of collaboration. This includes utilizing standardized tools (such as SBAR: Situation, Background, Assessment, Recommendation) to relay information concisely and accurately, ensuring all team members act on a shared, common understanding of the patient's status.

**Teams and Teamwork**
Successful clinical teams function through shared leadership, psychological safety, and mutual accountability. Teams must move beyond mere cooperation toward true collaboration, where decision-making is shared and centered on the patient's needs.

**Conflict Resolution**
Conflict is an inevitable component of high-stakes environments. The chapter identifies constructive approaches to conflict, emphasizing professional communication, active listening, and a focus on patient safety rather than personal grievances. Techniques such as the DESC (Describe, Express, Specify, Consequences) script are recommended for navigating difficult interpersonal interactions.

**Nursing Responsibilities**
Registered nurses serve as central figures in the interprofessional team, often acting as the primary link between the patient and other providers. The nurse is responsible for facilitating communication, evaluating the plan of care, and ensuring that collaborative efforts remain focused on the holistic needs of the patient.
Here is a quiz for this lesson.

questions.question: When prioritizing patient care, why is it essential for the nurse to utilize critical thinking and clinical reasoning instead of relying solely on a fixed list of tasks?
options: Clinical reasoning allows the nurse to interpret changing cues and adjust priorities based on the specific, real-time physiological status of each patient.
Critical thinking is only required when the nurse is supervising unlicensed assistive personnel, not when providing direct care.
Fixed lists are outdated and legally prohibited by most state Boards of Nursing.
The NCLEX-RN test plan mandates that nurses never use task-based checklists to organize their shifts.
questions.explanation: Prioritization is a dynamic process. Clinical reasoning allows nurses to analyze complex, changing patient conditions to determine which needs are most urgent, rather than simply working through a static list of duties.

questions.question: A staff nurse observes a conflict between two colleagues regarding patient care responsibilities. According to principles of interprofessional collaboration, what is the most effective initial professional approach to this conflict?
options: Ignore the conflict as it is a personal matter between staff members.
Report the colleagues to the Nursing Supervisor immediately to ensure a formal record is created.
Assign the work to a third party to avoid further emotional escalation in the unit.
Engage in direct, respectful communication with the involved parties to identify the root cause and seek a collaborative resolution.
questions.explanation: Effective conflict resolution in interprofessional practice relies on direct communication and addressing the underlying professional issues, which fosters teamwork and minimizes disruption to patient care.

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of delegating nursing tasks while maintaining supervisory responsibility?
options: To reduce the nurse's workload so they can focus exclusively on documentation and charting.
To eliminate the need for the RN to oversee the care provided by unlicensed assistive personnel.
To shift all legal accountability for potential medication errors to the delegatee.
To optimize patient outcomes by utilizing the specific scopes of practice of different team members efficiently.
questions.explanation: Delegation is a management tool used to match nursing tasks with the appropriate skill level of team members, ensuring safe and efficient care while the RN remains accountable for the overall outcome.

questions.question: When applying the concept of 'Resource Stewardship' in health care, what is the most appropriate action for a nurse?
options: Prioritizing care solely based on which patients have insurance funding that provides the highest reimbursement.
Withholding necessary diagnostic tests to ensure the unit stays under budget.
Following hospital policies even when the nurse knows a more cost-effective, evidence-based alternative exists.
Ensuring that supplies are used effectively and avoiding waste while still meeting the safety and quality standards of patient care.
questions.explanation: Resource stewardship involves balancing cost-conscious practices with high-quality, safe care. It focuses on the wise use of resources without compromising the patient's well-being.

questions.question: A new graduate nurse feels overwhelmed by the fast-paced environment and begins to experience signs of burnout. Based on the concept of 'Professional Management,' what is a proactive strategy to address this?
options: Engaging in self-care practices and utilizing institutional resources to mitigate stress and promote professional longevity.
Isolating oneself during breaks to recuperate without external stimuli.
Working additional shifts to master skills faster and reduce the feeling of incompetence.
Waiting for the organization to mandate a wellness program before seeking assistance.
questions.explanation: Burnout management is a professional responsibility. Proactive self-care and seeking support are essential strategies for maintaining mental health and professional standards throughout a nursing career.

cards.front: What is the primary purpose of the 'Nursing Management and Professional Concepts' textbook?
cards.back: To prepare prelicensure nursing students for graduation and the NCLEX-RN exam.

cards.front: Which regulatory documents and standards inform the content of this textbook?
cards.back: The 2023 NCLEX-RN Test Plan, the Wisconsin Technical College System (WTCS) nursing curriculum, and the Wisconsin Nurse Practice Act.

cards.front: What type of license is the 'Nursing Management and Professional Concepts' textbook released under?
cards.back: Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License (CC BY 4.0).

cards.front: What are the core areas covered in the chapters of this textbook?
cards.back: Prioritization, Delegation and Supervision, Leadership and Management, Legal Implications, Ethical Practice, Interprofessional Collaboration, Health Care Economics, Quality EBP, Advocacy, RN Role Preparation, and Burnout/Self-Care.

cards.front: What teaching tool is included in the textbook to help students develop clinical judgment?
What is included in the Appendix of the book to assist with classroom application?
cards.back: A 'suite of patients' with prompts for classroom discussion.

cards.front: What role does the 'Open RN' project play regarding these textbooks?
cards.back: It is an Open Educational Resource (OER) project that provides free, peer-reviewed nursing textbooks to ensure accessibility and quality.

cards.front: Who are the primary editors of the second edition?
cards.back: Kimberly Ernstmeyer and Dr. Elizabeth Christman.

cards.front: How does the textbook address student burnout?
cards.back: Chapter 12 focuses on identifying stress in self and others, understanding organizational stress, and mitigating it through self-care.

cards.front: What is the 'IPEC Core Competencies' concept mentioned in the text?
cards.back: It refers to the Interprofessional Education Collaborative competencies, which guide collaboration within the interprofessional health care team.

slides.title: Nursing Management & Professional Concepts
bullets: Comprehensive guide for prelicensure nursing students.
Aligned with 2023 NCLEX-RN and state practice standards.
slides.notes: This course covers essential management and leadership skills required for the modern registered nurse. It prepares students for clinical excellence and professional responsibility.

slides.title: Standards and Conceptual Approach
bullets: Guided by ANA practice standards and code of ethics.
Incorporates QSEN competencies for safe, quality care.
Uses a holistic framework to address social determinants.
Features case studies aligned with the NCSBN judgment model.
slides.notes: Professional nursing practice is built upon a foundation of evidence-based standards, aiming to improve health outcomes across diverse populations.

slides.title: Overview of Professional Practice
bullets: ANA defines nursing as the diagnosis/treatment of human responses.
Nurse Practice Acts dictate legal scope of practice (p. 20).
Boards of Nursing regulate licensure and disciplinary actions.
Agency policies and protocols provide specific operational guidance.
slides.notes: Nurses practice within a legal and professional framework that ensures safety and consistency in any healthcare environment.

slides.title: Professional Practice Standards
bullets: Standards address ethics, advocacy, and cultural humility.
Core competencies include effective communication and collaboration.
Leadership and scholarly inquiry emphasize professional growth.
Quality practice ensures resources are used for safe care.
slides.notes: The ANA sets clear standards for registered nurses to maintain professional accountability, continuous education, and high-quality patient outcomes.
